beats.activity: Wake up, brew tea, journal by the window in soft morning light.
beats.reasoning: Maya is an early riser who loves morning rituals; this aligns with her pattern of tea, journaling, and sunrise contemplation before the day begins.

beats.activity: Walk to Gas Works Park for sunrise meditation and stillness.
beats.reasoning: Gas Works is a cherished ritual for Maya; the calm sunny morning is perfect for her meditation practice, and it's a short walk from Fremont.

beats.activity: Walk back home and prepare for the day.
beats.reasoning: Return commute from Gas Works to Fremont to freshen up before heading to work; allows time for a quick shower and change.

beats.activity: Commute to CorePower Yoga for morning teaching shift.
beats.reasoning: Sunday morning class is typical for yoga instructors; Maya teaches back-to-back classes on weekends, so this is the start of her teaching day.

beats.activity: Teach a 75-minute yoga class.; Teach second yoga class of the day.
beats.reasoning: Standard yoga class duration; Sunday mornings are popular times for classes.

beats.activity: Grab a smoothie bowl and sit mindfully to eat, phone off.
beats.location_name: Solstice Wood Fired Pizza (Capitol Hill, Seattle)
beats.reasoning: Maya loves smoothie bowls and practices phone-free eating as a small rebellion; this aligns with her mindful approach and avoidance of screens before noon.

beats.activity: Stroll through Capitol Hill boutiques and shops, letting the mind settle.
beats.reasoning: Maya enjoys wandering Capitol Hill's boutiques on free afternoons; it's a calm, contemplative activity that fits her personality.
Head home before the afternoon heat builds; the day is sunny and warm, so she'll want to settle in for rest.

beats.activity: Gentle stretching on mat, read in golden hour light, restore after teaching.
beats.reasoning: Post-teaching restoration time; Maya practices gentle stretches and reading at home to recharge after multiple classes.

beats.activity: Walk through Woodland Park trails, noticing light through ferns and the quiet beauty of nature.
beats.reasoning: Nature walks are a core part of Maya's routine; Woodland Park is close to Fremont and offers the meditative outdoor experience she cherishes.

beats.activity: Return home and prepare a slow, intentional dinner with fresh vegetables.
beats.reasoning: Short walk back to Fremont from Woodland Park; transition into evening wind-down mode.

beats.activity: Cook and eat dinner slowly, sip tea, read, and wind down for the evening in a calm, restorative state.
beats.reasoning: Evening wind-down at home with cooking, tea, and reading—this prepares her for sleep and completes the day with the grounded, intentional feeling she values.
7-hour sleep span ending before dawn, allowing Maya to wake early for her cherished morning ritual; low-energy previous day suggests she needs good rest.

diary: Woke early to soft light filtering through my Fremont window, tea steaming in my hands while I journaled. The walk to Gas Works Park felt sacred—just me and the sunrise, the city still sleeping below. After teaching back-to-back classes at CorePower, I was buzzing with that post-teaching energy, so I grabbed a smoothie bowl at Solstice and wandered Capitol Hill's boutiques for a bit, letting my mind settle. The afternoon was pure restoration: golden light on my living room mat, then a long walk through Woodland Park where the ferns caught the sun in the most perfect way. I spent the last few hours cooking slowly—chopping vegetables with intention, sipping tea, reading—and honestly, I feel held. Like the whole day was designed to replenish exactly what I needed to give away in those morning classes.
thought_process: Today was one of those rare Sundays where everything aligned without effort. The weather was perfect, which always lifts my mood, and I was intentional about balancing my teaching energy with genuine restoration time. I didn't rush between beats like I usually do—I let the morning meditation settle in, took time to browse shops without agenda, and really honored the afternoon slowness. Teaching two classes back-to-back can leave me scattered, so I made sure to eat mindfully and move gently afterward. The walk through Woodland Park felt like the turning point where my nervous system finally released. By the time I was cooking dinner, I was genuinely present instead of mentally planning the week ahead. Sometimes the most productive thing is to do nothing at all.
world_event_prompt: Calm, sunny Sunday, high of 72F. Quiet end-of-week day with no major disruptions. Lake Union sees heavy recreational boat and paddle traffic in the afternoon.
